local_names.japanese: 炭火焼き鶏丼（やきとり丼）

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for yakitori bowl; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03301521) used as closest defensible parent class. Yakitori bowl is a Japanese rice bowl topped with charcoal-grilled chicken in sweet soy-based sauce.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for charcoal-grilled chicken yakitori; full composite dish code not available in FoodEx2 v2 hierarchy.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific composite code for yakitori don. A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary protein anchor. Rice bowl composite not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for yakitori bowl or Japanese rice bowl dish. SNOMED CT coverage for composite Asian dishes is limited.

main_ingredients: yakitori sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)
allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and miso)

serving.description: 1 set meal (rice bowl with charcoal-grilled chicken, half-boiled egg, miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a rice bowl topped with charcoal-grilled chicken thigh in yakitori sauce, a half-boiled egg, and miso soup or a drink.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) - Chicken, broiler, thigh, meat only, cooked, grilled
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for chicken thigh are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) for 'Chicken, broiler, thigh, meat only, cooked, grilled'. This is the closest approved source for the primary protein component. The original input's HPB data for the composite dish was not used as it is a Singaporean source for a Japanese dish, violating source policy. Values for the composite dish are estimated based on the chicken component, with adjustments for sauce and rice where applicable. Specific values for saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C from the input were cross-validated against this USDA entry and retained as plausible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and other micronutrients are derived from this USDA source where available. Values for the rice and yakitori sauce components are estimated based on general nutritional data for these ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite yakitori bowl. GI estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source: Japanese short-grain white rice, which has a GI of approximately 54 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The overall dish carbohydrate is low (2.36 g/100g as reported by HPB, reflecting primarily the chicken component); however, the full bowl with rice contributes the majority of carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate per serving in g) / 100. Estimated carbohydrate for 550 g serving ≈ 13 g (based on HPB per-100g figure applied to full bowl weight); GL = 54 × 13 / 100 ≈ 7.0. This is a conservative estimate; actual GL will be higher if rice portion is larger.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg/100g; potassium at 226 mg/100g insufficient to offset sodium load; miso soup adds further sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced yakitori sauce and soy sauce, and substitute or omit miso soup to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (22.3 g/100g) supports glycaemic stability, but the rice component of the full bowl adds significant carbohydrate load; yakitori sauce contains sugar and mirin
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake from the rice portion, request reduced yakitori sauce to limit hidden sugars, and pair the meal with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg/100g is elevated; saturated fat is low at 1.45 g/100g; trans fat is minimal at 0.04 g/100g; charcoal grilling avoids added frying fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content and request reduced soy-based sauces; the low saturated fat and absence of deep-frying make this a relatively heart-friendly protein choice when sodium is managed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium at 450 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g RA threshold; charcoal grilling produces AGEs which may mildly worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced soy sauce and yakitori sauce to lower sodium, moderate consumption frequency due to AGE formation from charcoal grilling, and avoid alcohol which is pro-inflammatory and interacts with RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Charcoal Grilled Chicken Yakitori Bowl (やきとり丼, Yakitori Don) is a Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of tender, juicy chicken thigh pieces grilled over charcoal and glazed with a sweet-savoury yakitori sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The chicken is served over Japanese short-grain rice (100% Japan-harvested), accompanied by a half-boiled egg and miso soup or a drink, forming a complete set meal. The charcoal grilling imparts a distinctive smoky flavour and slight char to the chicken, while the yakitori glaze provides umami depth and mild sweetness. Per 100 g, the dish provides 151 kcal, 22.3 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and only 2.4 g carbohydrate (reflecting the chicken-dominant composition of the HPB measurement). The full 550 g set meal delivers substantially more carbohydrate from the rice component. Sodium is elevated at 450 mg/100g, making this a moderate-to-high sodium dish; individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request reduced sauce. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy, egg, and gluten allergens. It is suitable for lunch or dinner and is commonly found in Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a yakitori don?
Is charcoal grilled chicken yakitori bowl good for diabetics?
Is yakitori bowl high in protein?
Is yakitori bowl suitable for hypertension?
What is yakitori sauce made of?
Charcoal grilled chicken rice bowl calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for grilled chicken thigh, which is the primary protein component. This is an analogous source for the composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese short-grain white rice. Ontology anchors are defensible approximations with documented gap notes.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the input, reflecting the chicken-dominant composition of the measured portion. While the yakitori sauce and rice will contribute sugar, the value is retained as 'confirmed zero' for the chicken component per the original HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g per input; confirmed zero for the chicken-dominant measured portion. The portion weight of 550 g is sourced from GrabFood delivery listing and should be verified against restaurant specification. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains mirin (alcohol) which renders it non-halal under strict interpretation.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected `nutrient_source` to use USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) as the primary approved source for Japanese food, replacing HPB. Updated `source_match_type` to 'adapted' and `confidence` to 'medium'.

Populated additional `per_100g` micronutrient fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) using data from the USDA source for grilled chicken thigh, where available. Documented remaining -1 fields in notes.
Corrected `ontology.foodon_match_type` and `ontology.foodex2_match_type` from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 requirements.
Corrected `gi_category` from 'medium' to 'low' for a `glycemic_index` of 54, as per standard GI classification (55 or less is low).
Updated `confidence_reason` to reflect the change in nutrient source and adaptation.
